Facts.text: Jewish autonomy in Palestine ended by the Romans and Raban Gamliel forced from office
Boniface II ends his reign as Catholic Pope
Monastary of Windesheim consecrated in the Netherlands
Cosma de' Migliorati elected Pope Innocentius VII
Second Battle of Kosovo, where the mainly Hungarian army led by John Hunyadi were defeated by an Ottoman army led by Sultan Murad II.
The University of Greifswald is established, making it the second oldest university in northern continental Europe (also for a period the oldest in Sweden, and Prussia)
Tomas de Torquemada appointed inquisitor-general of Spain
New royal charter for Massachusetts, now including Maine, Plymouth
Pierre de Marivaux's play "Arlequin Poli Par l'Amour" premieres in Paris
Ivan VI becomes Tsar of Russia
British General John Burgoyne surrenders at Saratoga during the American Revolutionary War
Boston African Americans petition legislature for equal school facilities
Peace of Campo Formio: Austrian Dutch possessions & France
Dutch colony Curacao transfererd to Great Britain
Former leader of the Haitian Revolution, Emperor Jacques I of Haiti is assassinated
Political rights of Jews suspended in Duchy of Warsaw
London Beer Flood: Bursting vat at Veux & Company Brewery floods city streets with over 300,000 gallons of porter ale, killing eight, plus a possible ninth later from alcohol poisoning
1st supposed attack on abandoned German teenager Kaspar Hauser
Delaware River & Chesapeake Bay Canal formally opens
Knickerbocker Engine Co Number 5 organized
French and British forces bombard Sevastopol for the first time during the Crimean War
Bessemer steelmaking process patented by Henry Bessemer - revolutionises manufacturing
Battle of Leetown & Thoroughfare Gap, VA
Constitution of Grand Duchy of Luxembourg comes into effect
Great Britain annexes Griqualand, South Africa
Baseball sets all players salaries at $1,000-$2,000 for 1885 season
Ohio national guard kills 3 lynchers while rescuing a black man
Anton Tsjechovs "Chayka" premieres in St Petersburg
Sutro railroad sold to Robert F Morrow for $215,000
Bulgaria, Greece & Serbia declares war on Turkey
Dutch women demonstrate for female suffrage
1st British bombing of Germany
De Kooy airport in Netherlands opens
Yugoslavia proclaims itself a republic
Radio Corporation of America (RCA) is created as a subsidiary of General Electric

Decatur Staleys, later known as Chicago Bears, play 1st American Professional Football Association game against an affiliated APFA team; beat Rock Island Independents, 7-0 at Douglas Park, Rock Island, Illinois
Belgium's public library law goes into force
Scottish worker begins hunger march from Glasgow on London
Catholic University of Nijmegen opens in the Netherlands
"The Aldrich Family" premieres on radio
Pacific Association of AAU votes not to participate in Berlin Olympics
German occupiers issue identity cards
USS Kearney becomes the 1st US destroyer torpedoed in World War II, while the country is still officially neutral
Liberators sink U-540 & U-631
Egyptian army fires on British troops
Philadelphia Eagle Adrian Burk passes for 7 touchdowns vs Wash (49-21)
Lee Merriwether joins Today Show panel
England's 1st large scale nuclear power station opens
Pakistan defeat Australia by nine wickets at 1st attempt
Dike Marken-Dutch mainland closed
II Tsjoendrigar becomes premier of Pakistan
Stinchcomb Memorial in Cleveland Metroparks' dedicated
US & Britain sign an accord granting US access to the British nuclear submarine base in Holy Loch, Scotland
22nd congress of CPSU opens in Moscow
Battle of Paris-police kill 210 Algerians
The Beatles make their live television debut, appearing on Manchester's local "People And Places" program, performing ‘Some Other Guy’ and their new single ‘Love Me Do’
The Beatles record "I Want to Hold Your Hand" at EMI Studios in London
In the first ever Olympic women's pentathlon Irina Press of the Soviet Union sets a world record 5,246 points to win the gold medal at the Tokyo Games
US swimmers go 1-2-3 in the women's 400m individual medley at the Tokyo Olympics; Donna de Varona wins gold in Olympic record 5:18.7 ahead of teammates Sharon Finneran and Martha Randall
WBMG TV channel 42 in Birmingham, AL (CBS) begins broadcasting
"Hair" premieres on Broadway
USSR performs nuclear test at the Semipalatinsk Test Site in northeast Kazakhstan
William "Pete" Knight in X-15 reaches 85 km
Björn Ferm of Sweden beats Hungary's András Balczó by just 11 points to take the gold medal in the modern pentathlon at the Mexico City Olympics
Soviet athlete Viktor Saneev sets world record 17.39m to win the triple jump at the Mexico City Olympics; world record improved 5 times by 3 different athletes during competition
NY Nets move from Comack to Island Garden, Hempstead NY
Plastic Ono Band's "Cold Turkey" is released in UK
Soyuz 7 returns to Earth
Cleveland Metroparks' Rocky River Nature Center opens

It is estimated today that approximately 16,000 households were withholding rent and rates for council houses as part of the campaign of civil disobedience against internment organised by the Social Democratic and Labour Party, Northern Ireland
Bob Randall's "6 Rooms Riv Vu," premieres in NYC
The Ulster Defence Association open fire on the British Army in several areas of Belfast
OPEC oil ministers use oil as an economic weapon in the Arab-Israeli War, mandating a cut in exports and recommending an embargo against unfriendly states
NBA New Orleans Jazz begin a 28-game road losing streak against the Knicks in New York City; their first road victory doesn't come until February
NHL Washington Capitals 1st franchise home victory, beating Chicago Blackhawks, 4-3
1st Space Shuttle main engine test at National Space Tech Labs, at Pearl River, Mississippi
UN drafts a resolution for consideration saying, in part, "Zionism is a form of racism"
China performs nuclear test at Lop Nor, PRC
Canada begins regular live TV coverage of Parliament
West German commandos storm hijacked Lufthansa in Mogadishu, Somalia freeing all 86 hostages & killing 3 of 4 hijackers
NY Islanders start a streak of 23 undefeated games at home (15-0-8)
USSR performs underground nuclear test
Yanks win 22nd World Championship capping their great comeback year
D Bautista of Mexico completes 20,000 m walk in record 1:20:06.8
US PresidentJimmy Cartersigns legislation creating Department of Education
1st live orchestra on a US commercial network since 1954 (National Symphony)
USSR performs nuclear test at Novaya Zemlya USSR
Nobel Prize in Economics awarded to Gérard Debreu
STS-9 vehicle moves to Vehicle Assembly Building at Kennedy Space Center (Florida) due to SRB nozzle problem
Nobel prize for chemistry awarded to Bruce Merrifield
French author Claude Simon wins the Nobel Prize for Literature
Lou Piniella named NY Yankee manager
US Senate approved immigration bill prohibiting hiring of illegal aliens & offered amnesty to illegals who entered prior to 1982
31 reported dead as Ugandan jetliner crashes in fog near Rome
Dallas Green replaces Lou Pinella as manager of NY Yankees
Lyndon LaRouche pleads innocent to fraud, conspiracy indictment
Phillip Morris announces $11 Billion tender offer for Kraft
Blue Man Group's 1st performance in New York City
News anchor Bree Walker Lampley files an FCC complaint that LA radio KFI-AM personally attacked her by discussing her having a disformed baby
Billy Joelperforms opening concert at Cleveland's Gund Arena
Keith Moore sentenced to 6 years for robbing Sting of $9,000,000
"Taking Sides" opens at Atkinson Theater NYC
At Jesse, in the Niger Delta, Nigeria, a petroleum pipeline explodes killing about 1200 villagers, some of whom are scavenging gasoline.
Train crash at Hatfield, north of London, leading to collapse of Railtrack
Eunuchs in the Indian state of Madhya Pradesh float the political party Jiti Jitayi Politics.

Pinnacle fitted on the roof of Taipei 101, a 101-floor skyscraper in Taipei, allowing it to surpass the Petronas Twin Towers in Kuala Lumpur by 50 meters (165 feet) to become World's tallest high-rise
The United States population reaches 300 million
Iran's attempt to create the world's largest sandwich (1,500 metres) fails when crowds eat it before it can be measured
The Teshima Art Museum by architect Ryue Nishizawa and artist Rei Naito opens on the island of Teshima, Japan
Exoplanet Alpha Centauri Bb discovered orbiting Alpha Centauri announced (later thought to be a false finding)
Tens of thousands protest against austerity measures in Greece
59 people are killed in a wave of attacks on Shia Muslims in Iraq
Chinese spacecraft Shenzhou-11 launched from Jiuquan Satellite Launch Centre in northern China
American short-story writer George Saunders wins the Man Booker Prize for his first novel "Lincoln in the Bardo"
Islamic State headquarters in Raqqa is declared under full control of the US-led alliance by Syrian Democratic Forces (SDF) spokesperson Talal Sello after four months of fighting
Australian state of Queensland decriminalizes abortion
Big Bird and Oscar the Grouch puppeteer Caroll Spinney leaves "Sesame Street" after 50 years
Canada legalizes the sale of recreational cannabis, the second country after Uruguay
India's junior foreign minister MJ Akbar is highest official to resign in #MeToo case after sexual harassment accusations by numerous women
Student shoots and detonates a bomb killing 20 with 40 injured at Kerch polytechnic college, Crimea
The 'Blob', mysterious yellow slime organism (physarum polycephalum) with 720 sexes, moves and can solve problems to go on display at the Paris Zoological Park
US adults identifying as Christian falls 12% in a decade to 2/3 according to the Pew Research Center, born-again Protestants falls to 16%
Chicago declared the 'rattiest city' in America for the sixth year in a row by pest control service Orkin
Shehan Karunatilka is the second Sri Lankan author to win the Booker Prize with his novel "The Seven Moons of Maali Almeida"
India's Supreme Court rules against legalising same-sex marriage and adoption for queer couples
Teen smoking in the US falls to its lowest level in 25 years, decreasing 20% in a year from 2.8 million to 2.25 million, including e-cigarettes
Battle of Neville's Cross: King David II of Scotland is captured byEdward IIIof England at Calais, and imprisoned in the Tower of London for eleven years
Nine Regicides, the men who signed the death warrant ofCharles I, are hanged, drawn and quartered, another is hanged.
Charles IIof Great Britain sells Dunkirk to France for 2.5 million livres (320,000 English pounds)
CaptainJames Cookarrives in Cape Town with Resolution on his third trip to the Pacific Ocean
1st opera byFranz Liszt, "Don Sanche" premieres in Paris
Felix Mendelssohn's 1st Piano concert in G, premieres in Munich, Germany, with the composer as soloist
1st British Open Men's Golf, Prestwick GC:Willie Park Sr.wins the inaugural event by two strokes over fellow Scot Tom Morris Sr.
US PresidentUlysses S. Grantsuspends habeas corpus in parts of South Carolina during prosecutions against Ku Klux Klan
Henry Morton Stanleyreaches Lualaba River, headstream of the Congo River, goes on to travel down it proving  it is not the source of the Nile
After serving as the opposition for five years,John A. Macdonaldis re-elected as Prime Minister of Canada
Thomas Edisonfiles a patent for the Optical Phonograph (the first movie)
Bernhard von Bülowbecomes German Chancellor
Bank of Italy (Bank of America), founded byAmadeo Giannini, opens its doors in Jackson Square, San Francisco
Guglielmo Marconi's company begins the first commercial transatlantic wireless service between Glace Bay, Nova Scotia, Canada and Clifden, Ireland
Ban Johnson, in failing health, retires as president of MLB's American League
Albert Einsteinarrives in US as a refugee from Nazi Germany
"Mr. Smith Goes to Washington", directed byFrank Capraand starringJames Stewartand Jean Arthur, is released
Loyalty Day in Argentina, mass demonstrations held to releaseJuan Perón
"Around the World in 80 Days", based on the book byJules Verne, directed by Michael Anderson and starring David Nivon andCantinflas, premieres in New York
Chess "Game of the Century": 13-year-oldBobby Fischerdefeats 1953 U.S. Champion Donald Byrne in the Rosenwald Memorial Tournament at the Marshall Chess Club in New York City
Britain's Queen Elizabeth &Prince Philipvisit the White House
French authorAlbert Camusawarded Nobel Prize in Literature
Queen Elizabeth IIis fined $140 for withdrawing her race horse
NASA civilian pilotJoseph A. Walkertakes X-15 to 33,100 m
Australian athleteBetty Cuthberttakes her 4th career Olympic gold medal as she wins the women's 400m at the Tokyo Games in Olympic record 52.0
Barbra Streisandstars on "Belle of 14th Street" special on CBS
Memorial service forBrian Epsteinat New London Synagogue
"Bullitt" directed by Peter Yates and starringSteve McQueenand Jacqueline Bisset is 1st released

US men's 4 x 100m freestyle relay team of Zac Zorn, Stephen Rerych, Ken Walsh &Mark Spitzswim world record 3:31.7 to outclass the Soviet Union & Australia and win the gold medal at the Mexico City Olympics
Anwar Sadatsworn in as the 3rd President of Egypt
Chuck Berry's "My Ding-a-Lng," is #1
US PresidentJimmy Carterpresents Congressional Medal to American opera singerMarian Anderson
US PresidentJimmy Cartersigns bill restoring US citizenship to Civil War era Confederacy President Jefferson Davis
Mother Teresaof Calcutta awarded Nobel Peace Prize

BrazilianNelson Piquetdriving for Brabham finishes 5th in the season ending Caesars Palace Grand Prix in Las Vegas to clinch his first Formula 1 World Drivers Championship by 1 point from Carlos Reutemann
Talk show "TheSally Jessy RaphaelShow" with Sally Jessy Raphael debuts on KSDK
Yitzhak Rabinforms Israeli government
US First LadyNancy Reaganundergoes a modified radical mastectomy
Traveling Wilburys release debut album "Volume 1", featuring the single "Handle With Care"; pseudonymous band members includeRoy Orbison,Bob Dylan,George Harrison, Jeff Lynne, andTom Petty
Columbia Records releases "Storm Front", singer-songwriterBilly Joel's eleventh studio album
Green Day singerBillie Joe Armstrongdrops out of school to pursue a career in music
Ángel Cordero Jr.is 3rd jockey to win 7,000 horse races
Pitts PenguinPaul Coffeysets NHL defenseman scoring record with 1,053 career points (309 goals & 744 assists)
Kapil Dev's final one-day international (v West Indies)
"The Colbert Report" hosted byStephen Colbertfirst airs on US TV
Thierry Henrybecomes Arsenal's all-time leading scorer with two goals against Sparta Prague in the Champions League
TheDalai Lamareceives the United States Congressional Gold Medal
Sachin Tendulkarbecomes the highest run-scorer ever in test cricket beatingBrian Lara's record and passing 12,000 runs against Australia in Mohali
Brother André is the first Canadian to be canonized post Federation by PopeBenedict XVI
Lance Armstrongloses a host of endorsements in the wake of his doping scandal
Amy Schumer's comedy special "Amy Schumer: Live at the Apollo" premieres on HBO
Star forwardCristiano Ronaldobecomes Real Madrid's all-time leading scorer across all competitions, overtaking club legend Raul with his 324th goal in a 3-0 win over Levante
George Sorosannounces he has donated $18 billion to his grantmaking network the Open Society Foundations
Philippine PresidentRodrigo Dutertedeclares city of Marawi "liberated" from militants
The revived sitcom "The Conners" debuts on ABC TV starring Sara Gilbert, Laurie Metcalf, andJohn Goodman, without fired former starRoseanne Barr
Lana Del Rey's ballad "Video Games" named song of the decade at Q Awards in London
Mick Mulvaney,President Trump's acting chief of staff says the White House withheld nearly $400 million in military aid from Ukraine to further Trump's own political interests
Turkey agrees to a ceasefire in Northern Syria for 5 days to allow withdrawal of Kurdish troops, after talks between US Vice PresidentMike Penceand Turkish President Recep TayyipErdogan
UK Prime MinisterBoris Johnsonannounces new Brexit deal with the EU, removes the Northern Ireland backstop clause
Labour Party headed byJacinda Ardernre-elected in a landslide in New Zealand's general election
FIFA Ballon d'Or: Real Madrid strikerKarim Benzemawins his first award; Barcelona midfielder Alexia Putellas claims women's award for a second-straight season
K-pop bandBTSannounces that all seven members will be serving in the South Korean military for the required 18 month minimum
China's PresidentXi Jinpingcelebrates 10 years of its Belt and Rod project at summit in Beijing attended by Russian leaderVladimir Putin
Israel claims to have killed Hamas leaderYahya Sinwar, one of the masterminds of the 7 Oct Hamas attack on Israel, during battle in Rafah, Gaza
Burma Railway built by Allied POWs and Asian laborers is completed for use by the Japanese army
Musical film "Jailhouse Rock" starringElvis Presleypremieres in Memphis, Tennessee
Ivo of Kermartin, Catholic saint and patron of Brittany, born in Kermartin, Duchy of Brittany (d. 1303)
Jodocus Hondius, Flemish cartographer and engraver (publicized Drake's voyage, restored Mercator's reputation), born in Wakken, West Flanders (d. 1612)
Cristofano Allori, Italian Mannerist painter (Judith), born in Florence, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (now Italy) (d. 1621)
Johann Gerhard, German Lutheran leader and author of "Loci Theologici", born in Quedlinburg (d. 1637)
Francis Turretin, Swiss theologian, born in Geneva, Republic of Geneva
Domenico Zipoli, Italian organist and composer, born in Prato, Grand Duchy of Tuscany (d. 1726)
August III, King of Poland and Grand Duke of Lithuania (1733-63), Elector of Saxony, born in Dresden, Electorate of Saxony, Holy Roman Empire (d. 1763)
William Bentinck, British-born Dutch politician, prominent Orangist, and nobleman (Lord of Rhoon and Pendrecht, and Reichsgraf), born in London, England (d. 1774)  [Nov, 6 OS]
Jupiter Hammon, American slave and poet, 1st African American to publish poetry, born in Lloyd Harbor, New York (d. ca. 1806)
Jacques Cazotte, French writer and support of the monarchy, born in Dijon, France (d. 1792)
Maria Teresa Agnesi, Italian harpsichodist, singer, and composer, born in Milan, Habsburg Empire (d. 1795)
Pierre-Alexandre Monsigny, French opera composer (La belle Arsène), born in Fauquembergues, France (d. 1817)
Ernestus Weinrauch, German composer (Cain and Abel), organist, and Benedictine priest, born in Donauwörth, Kingdom of Bavaria (d. 1793)
Grigori Orlov, Russian military officer and favorite of Catherine the Great (helped her overthrow Peter III), born in Bezhetsky Uyezd, Russia (d. 1783) [NS]
Albertus van Raalte, Dutch-American Reformed vicar (settled Holland, Michigan), born in Wanneperveen, Netherlands (d. 1876)
Georg Büchner, German playwright, known for " Woyzeck,", born in Goddelau, Grand Duchy of Hesse (d. 1837)
Emanuel Geibel, German poet and playwright (Gedichte), born in the Free Imperial City of Lübeck (d. 1884)
Samuel Ringgold Ward, American Black abolitionist, minister and orator (Autobiography of a Fugitive Negro), born in Maryland (d. 1866)
Syed Ahmad Khan, Indian Muslim intellectual and one of the founding fathers of the Pakistan movement, born in Delhi, Mughal Empire (d. 1898)

Paul Bert, French Zoologist, Physiologist and pioneer of aerospace medicine whose study of the effects of air pressure on the body, such as altitude sickness and 'the bends', made possible space and ocean exploration, born in Auxerre, Burgundy (d. 1886)
Yaa Asantewaa, Ghanaian queen of Ashanti Empire, led fight against British colonialism, born in Besease, Ghana (d. 1921) appropriate date
Miguel Nieto, Spanish composer, born in Reus, Spain (d. 1915)
Franz Ehrle, German Jesuit and Vatican librarian (modernised the Vatican Library), born in  Isny im Allgäu, Kingdom of Württemberg, (d. 1934)
William "Candy" Cummings, American Baseball Hall of Fame pitcher (credited with creating the curveball), born in Ware, Massachusetts (d. 1924)
Maria Alexandrovna, Grand Duchess of Russia, who married into the British royal family, born at Alexander Palace, Tsarskoye Selo, Russia (d. 1920)
William "Buck" Ewing, American Baseball HOF catcher (NL home run leader 1883 NY Giants; Cincinnati Reds) and manager (NY Giants, Cincinnati Reds), born in Hoagland, Ohio (d. 1906)
Dionyssios Lavrangas, Greek conductor, composer, and pedagogue, born in Argostólion, Greece (d. 1915)
Elinor Glyn, British novelist (3 Weeks) and screenwriter, born in Jersey, Channel Islands (d. 1943)
James Rudolph Garfield, American politician, son of President Garfield who served under Theodore Roosevelt, born in Hiram, Ohio (d. 1950)
Alfred Polgar, Austrian writer and theater critic (Yes & No!), born in Vienna, Austria (d. 1955)
Barlow Carkeek, Australian cricket wicket-keeper (6 Tests, 6 dismissals; Victoria), born in Walhalla, Australia (d. 1937)
Henri Mulet, French organist, cellist, and composer (Carillon-Sortie; Esquisses Byzantines), born in Paris, France (d. 1967)
Floyd Smith, American aviator and inventor of the modern parachute, born in Geneseo, Illinois (d. 1956)[1]
Lewis Bennison, American actor (The Road Called Straight), born in Oakland, California (d. 1929)
Aaf Bouber [Aaf ten Hoope], Dutch actress (Bleeke Bet, Jantjes) and wife of Herman Bouber, born in Hoorn, Noord-Holland, Netherlands (d. 1974)
Spring Byington, American actress (December Bride), born in Colorado Springs (d. 1971)
Roy Kilner, English cricket all-rounder (9 Tests, 2 x 50, 24 wickets, BB 4/58; Yorkshire CCC), born in Low Valley, Wombwell, England (d. 1928)
Herbert Howells, British organist, composer (chiefly of Anglican Church sacred music), and teacher, born in Lydney, Gloucestershire England (d. 1983)
Otakar Jeremiáš, Czech composer and conductor, born in Písek, Kingdom of Bohemia (d. 1962)
Theodor Eicke, German Nazi official who developed the concentration camps, born in Hampont, German Empire (d. 1943)
Fritz Ascher, German Expressionist artist, and poet, persecuted by the Nazi regime, born in Berlin, German Empire (d. 1970)[1]
Jean Binet, Swiss composer, and educator (Jaques-Dalcroze method of experiencing music through movement), born in Geneva, Switzerland (d. 1960)
Doris Humphrey, American dancer and choreographer (Dances of Women), born in Oak Park, Illinois (d. 1958)
Frank Forest [Hayek], American actor (Big Broadcast of 1937, Champagne Waltz), born in St. Paul, Minnesota (d. 1976)
Mildred O. Knopf, American cookbook writer (Food of Italy), born in New York (d. 1993)
Shinichi Suzuki, Japanese music teacher (Suzuki Method), born in Nagoya, Japan (d. 1998)
Simon Vestdijk, Dutch writer and poet (5th Seal), born in Harlingen, Netherlands (d. 1971)
Jean Arthur [Gladys Greene], American stage, silent and sound screen actress (Shane; Mr. Smith Goes to Washington; Mr. Deeds Goes to Town), born in Plattsburgh, New York (d. 1991)
Theodoro Valcarcel, Peruvian classical composer, born in Puno, Peru (d. 1942)
Irene Ryan [Noblitt], American actress (Beverly Hillbillies - "Granny"; Pippin), born in El Paso, Texas (d. 1973)
Nathanael West, American novelist ("Miss Lonelyhearts"; "The Day of the Locust"), born in New York City (d. 1940)
Franco Albini, Italian Rationalist architect, industrial and furniture designer (Albini desk), born in Robbiate, Italy (d. 1977)
Paul Derringer, American baseball pitcher (6 × MLB All-Star; World Series 1931 St. Louis Cardinals, 1940 Cincinnati Reds), born in Springfield, Kentucky (d. 1987)
John Marley [Mortimer Marlieb], American actor (Cat Ballou; Love Story; The Godfather), born in New York City (d. 1984)
Red Rolfe, American baseball third baseman (5 x World Series; 4 x MLB All Star; NY Yankees) and manager (Detroit Tigers 1949–52), born in Penacook, New Hampshire (d. 1969)
(William) "Cozy" Cole, American jazz drummer ("Topsy I"; "Topsy II"), and educator, born in East Orange, New Jersey (d. 1981)
John Paul I [Albino Luciano], 263rd Roman Catholic pope (1978), born in Canale d'Agordo, Belluno, Veneto, Italy (d. 1978)
Albert Markos, Romanian composer (Torso I), born in Székelykeresztúr, Hungary (now Cristuru Secuiesc, Romania) (d. 1981)
Jerry Siegel, American comic book writer (co-creator, with Joe Shuster, of "Superman"), born in Cleveland, Ohio (d. 1996)
Robert Lowery [Hank], American stage and screen actor (Batman and Robin (1949); Circus Boy - "Big Tim"; The Rise and Fall of Legs Diamond), born in Kansas City, Missouri (d. 1971)
Tadeusz Paciorkiewicz, Polish organist and composer, born in Sierpc, Poland (d. 1998)
Marsha Hunt, American actress (Born to the West; Cry 'Havoc'; Johnny Got His Gun), and human rights and peace activist, born in Chicago, Illinois (d. 2022)
Martin Donnelly, New Zealand cricket batsman (7 Tests, 1 x 100, 4 x 50, HS 206; Wellington, Canterbury) and rugby union centre (1 Test England 1947), born in Ngāruawāhia, New Zealand (d. 1999)
Sumner Locke Elliott, Australian-born American novelist, known for "Careful He Might Hear You", born in Sydney (d. 1991)
Isaak Markovich Khalatnikov, Russian physicist, born in Ekaterinoslav (now Dnipro), Ukraine (d. 2021)
Miguel Delibes, Spanish novelist (The Holy Innocents), born in Valladodid, Spain (d. 2010)
Rudolf Hrusinský, Czech actor and director (Short Cut, Golden Eels), born in Nová Včelnice (d. 1994)
George Mackay Brown, Scottish poet and playwright, born in Stromness, Orkney Islands, Scotland (d. 1996)
Maria Gorokhovskaya, Russian gymnast (equal record 7 x Olympic medals, Soviet Union 1952; 2 x gold [team, all-round], 5 x silver), born in Yevpatoria, Russia (d. 2001)
Priscilla Buckley, American editor and columnist (National Review), born in New York City (d. 2012)
Tom Poston, American actor (Newhart - "George Utley"; Mork & Mindy - "Franklin Bickley"; The Bob Newhart Show; The Steve Allen Show), born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 2007)
Louis Benjamin, British showman, organized Royal Variety performances, born in London, England (d. 1994)
Luiz Bonfá, Brazilian guitarist and composer (Black Orpheus), born in Rio de Janeiro, Brazil (d. 2001)
Pierre Juneau, Canadian film and broadcast executive, born in Montreal, Quebec (d. 2012)
Barney Kessel, American jazz and session guitarist (The Wrecking Crew), born in Muskogee, Oklahoma (d. 2004)
Don Coryell, American College/Pro Football HOF coach (NCAA Division champion 1966, 67, 68 San Diego State; St. Louis Cardinals, SD Chargers), born in Seattle, Washington (d. 2010)
Beverly Garland [Fessenden], American actress (My Three Sons, Scarecrow and Mrs. King), born in Santa Cruz, California (d. 2008)
Julie Adams [Betty May Adams], American actress (Creature from the Black Lagoon; Code Red), born in Waterloo, Iowa (d. 2019)
Karl Gordon Henize, American astronomer and NASA astronaut (STS-51-F), born in Cincinnati, Ohio (d. 1993)
Ivan Marinov, Bulgarian orchestral and opera conductor, composer, and pedagogue, born in  Sofia, Bulgaria (d. 2003)
Jim Gilliam, American MLB player and coach (Dodgers, NL rookie of year 1953), born in Nashville, Tennessee (d. 1978)
Ram Da-Oz [Abraham Daus], Israeli composer, born in Berlin, Germany (d. 2021)
Angelo Paccignini, Italian composer (Flou II), educator (Milan Conservatory - electronic music chair. 1969-80), and musical director (Nuova Armonia), born in Castano Primo, Italy (d. 2001)

Jimmy Breslin, American investigative journalist, Pulitzer Prize-winning columnist (NY Post; Daily News: Newsday), and novelist (The Gang That Couldn't Shoot Straight), born in New York City (d. 2017)
Robert Atkins, American cardiologist, known for the Atkins diet, born in Columbus, Ohio (d. 2003)
Ernst Hinterberger, Austrian playwright and detective writer, born in Vienna, Austria (d. 2012)
Gundaris Poné, Latvian-American violinist, composer, and educator (NYU-New Paltz, 1964-94), born in Riga, Latvia (d. 1994)
Paul Anderson, American weightlifter (Olympic gold +90kg 1956; World C'ship gold 1955), born in Toccoa, Georgia (d. 1994)
William J. Hughes, American politician and diplomat (Rep-D-New Jersey, 1975-95), born in Salem, New Jersey (d. 2019)
Bill Anders, American USAF Major General, and NASA astronaut (Apollo 8), born in British Hong Kong (d. 2024)[1]
Jeanine Deckers, Belgian singer-songwriter, and Dominican nun known as 'The Singing Nun' ("Dominique"), born in Laeken, Belgium (d. 1985)
(Donald) "Der" Scutt, American architect (Grand Hyatt; One Astor Place; Trump Tower), born in Wyomissing, Pennsylvania (d. 2010)
Barend P Tammeling, Dutch journalist (Trouw, Groninger Gezinsbode), born in Groningen, Netherlands (d. 1993)
Emmanuel "Rico" Rodriguez, Jamaican ska trombonist (The Specials), born in Kingston, Jamaica (d. 2015)
Johnny Haynes, English soccer forward (56 caps; Fulham 594 games), born in London, England (d. 2005)
David Hobson, American politician (Rep-R-Ohio 1991-2009), born in Cincinnati, Ohio
Hiroo Kanamori, Japanese seismologist known for his work on earthquakes, born in Japan
Paxton Whitehead, English stage and screen actor (Back to School, Mad About You - "Hal"), born in East Malling, Kent (d. 2023)
Jim Smith, English soccer midfielder and manager (Birmingham City, QPR, Portsmouth, Derby County), born in Sheffield, South Yorkshire (d. 2019)
Peter Stringfellow, British nightclub owner (Stringfellows), born in Sheffield, England (d. 2018)
Alan Howard, British rock bassist (Brian Poole & The Tremeloes - "Do You Love Me?"), born in  Bromley, England
Earl Thomas Conley, American singer-songwriter with 18 No. 1 country hits ("Once In A Blue Moon"; "Angel In Disguise"; Too Many Times"), born in Portsmouth, Ohio (d. 2019)
Jim Seals, American singer-songwriter, guitarist, and fiddler (Seals & Crofts - "Summer Breeze"), born in Sidney, Texas (d. 2022)
Viden Apostolov, Bulgarian soccer defender (22 caps; PFC Botev Plovdiv 429 games), born in Novi Iskar, Bulgaria (d. 2020)
Gary Puckett, American pop vocalist (Gary Puckett & The Union Gap - "Woman, Woman"; "Lady Willpower"), born in Hibbing, Minnesota
Susan Davenny Wyner, American music director, conductor (Warren Philharmonic, 1999-; Opera Western Reserve, 2004-), and operatic soprano (Walter Naumberg Prize, 1973), born in New Haven, Connecticut
